docs.title: Hello! 👋🏾 I'm Juan
docs.text: (pronounced as /hwɑːn/). And yes, that's me in the picture 😄

docs.title: A little about me.
docs.text: I'm a tech problem-solver who enjoys diving into challenging projects. Whether it's hardware or software, I love finding creative ways to tackle complex challenges. Every new project is a chance to grow, and I'm always ready to jump in, adapt, and bring something unique to the table. For me, work isn't just about getting things done. It's about pushing boundaries and enjoying the journey of creating something meaningful and impactful.

docs.title: About what I do.
docs.text: These are some of my projects. Enjoy!

docs.title: Autodesk Gallery 2.0
docs.text:
Lightning electric motorcycle AR 3

This project presented several technical challenges to fulfill the client's specifications regarding AR experience activation. The requirement was to create a completely seamless interaction, eliminating visible markers and instead utilizing only the physical objects themselves as triggers. This approach demanded innovative solutions to maintain both functionality and user experience quality.

I worked on 3 different applications for this project:

  • The Lightning electric motorcycle, where I used the bike as a
  • The NEST Digital Twin
  • The LEVVEL project in Afsluitdijk, Netherlands

docs.title: Twitter enabled candy machine
docs.text:
Candy Machine Mov

The Twitter enabled candy machine dispenses a sweet treat when users tweet using the hashtag #projectacme. It was a one day project before the annual project showcase at the Centre for Digital Media.

docs.text: Arduino, Raspberry Pi, Servomotors, Twitter (X) API, Python

docs.title: Cell Phone Disco
docs.text: This small project involved creating a modular signage system using Open exhibits, an open source multi-touch SDK designed specifically for museums. Open exhibits is powered by GestureWorks and Tangible Engine developed by Ideum.

docs.title: Craigdarroch AR
docs.text: For this project, we created an iOS content delivery system based on indoor location. It also features three different Augmented Reality scenes for different rooms inside the castle.

docs.title: Videos
docs.text: Craigdarroch Augmented Reality. Courtesy of NGX Interactive

docs.title: Technical Environment
docs.text: Unity3D, iOS, Estimote Bluetooth LE beacons, Vuforia SDK

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: National Association for Interpretation (NAI) – Interpretive Media Awards, Digital Media Category, 2nd place. 2017

docs.title: Exquisite Corpse
docs.text:
For the exquisite corpse, the team decided to create a collaborative space as a creative outlet for the students of our master’s program.

This short time-lapse video created with screen captures of the website, shows what we collected during the course of about a week:

Here’s an extract of the original description that was included in the website:

The exquisite corpse is a private shared space for the 54 students of the cdm’s cohort 9. We sought to address the need of artistic forms of social communication. We saw this project as an opportunity to build an elegant and compelling experience.

Please visit the blog for more information.

docs.title: My Ocean World
docs.text:
My Ocean World

Using an Intel RealSense camera, I developed an object detection system in C++ to track the user's arms on the table. This way the users can interact with the objects projected on the table.

docs.title: Bird Watch
docs.text:
Látrabjarg Cliff Model

I created an immersive augmented reality project that simulated the experience of observing scenic cliff views within an indoor environment. Using a combination of rotary encoders and cameras, visitors could discover hidden layers of information and visual effects while interacting with a physical recreation of a cliff.

The installation featured a detailed indoor recreation of the cliff formation, meticulously crafted to capture the geological features and textures. When users used the AR visors, the application using the data from the encoders would trigger specific animations, seamlessly blending digital content with the physical structure.

The system provided a unique and rare perspective that made visitors feel as if they were at the base of the cliff looking straight up—a truly stunning view that would typically be inaccessible or dangerous to experience in real-world settings.

The precision of the rotary encoders created an intuitive interface between the physical and digital elements of the experience. This tangible interaction helped ground users in the physical space while they explored the augmented features, creating a more engaging and memorable experience than purely virtual approaches.

The indoor setting allowed for controlled lighting and environmental conditions, ensuring optimal visibility for both the physical model and the AR overlays regardless of time of day or weather, while still delivering the awe-inspiring sense of scale and perspective of the real cliff formation.

docs.title: Digital Sandbox
docs.text: We process the point cloud data from a Kinect V2 in Unity to create the colour gradient that is projected on the sand. This was my first project at NGX Interactive.
